\$(\$node.Name) \[\$(\$node.Id)\] \| Path: \$(\$node.Path)" \# ------------------------------------------------------- \# STEP 1: Ensure the dynamic group exists \# ------------------------------------------------------- \$existingGroup = Get-MgGroup \` -Filter "displayName eq '\$groupName'" \` -Property "Id,DisplayName,MembershipRule,MembershipRuleProcessingState" \` -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue if (-not \$existingGroup) { \$groupBody = @{ DisplayName = \$groupName Description = \$description MailEnabled = \$false MailNickname = \$mailNick SecurityEnabled = \$true GroupTypes = @("DynamicMembership") MembershipRule = \$memberRule MembershipRuleProcessingState = "On" } if (\$DryRun) { Write-Host " \[DRYRUN\] Would create group: \$groupName" Write-Host " Rule: \$memberRule" \$results.GroupsCreated++ continue } try { \$newGroup = New-MgGroup -BodyParameter \$groupBody Write-Host " \[CREATED\] Group: \$groupName \[\$(\$newGroup.Id)\]" \$groupId = \$newGroup.Id \$results.GroupsCreated++ } catch { Write-Warning " \[FAILED\] Could not create group '\$groupName': \$\_" \$results.Failed++ continue } } else { \$groupId = \$existingGroup.Id Write-Host " \[EXISTS\] Group: \$groupName \[\$groupId\]" \# Validate membership rule is current — update if the node's path has changed if (\$existingGroup.MembershipRule -ne \$memberRule) { Write-Host " \[UPDATE\] Membership rule updated for \$groupName" if (-not \$DryRun) { Update-MgGroup -GroupId \$groupId -MembershipRule \$memberRule } } \$results.GroupsExisting++ } \# ------------------------------------------------------- \# STEP 2: Assign Azure RBAC role to the group at scope \# ------------------------------------------------------- \$existingAssignment = Get-AzRoleAssignment \` -ObjectId \$groupId \` -RoleDefinitionName \$RoleDefinitionName \` -Scope \$Scope \` -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue if (-not \$existingAssignment) { if (\$DryRun) { Write-Host " \[DRYRUN\] Would assign '\$RoleDefinitionName' at scope \$Scope for \$groupName" \$results.RbacAssigned++ continue } try { New-AzRoleAssignment \` -ObjectId \$groupId \` -RoleDefinitionName \$RoleDefinitionName \` -Scope \$Scope \| Out-Null Write-Host " \[ASSIGNED\] '\$RoleDefinitionName' at \$Scope for \$groupName" \$results.RbacAssigned++ } catch { Write-Warning " \[FAILED\] RBAC assignment failed for \$groupName at \$Scope\`: \$\_" \$results.Failed++ } } else { Write-Host " \[EXISTS\] RBAC assignment already in place for \$groupName" } } \# --------------------------------------------------------------- \# SUMMARY \# --------------------------------------------------------------- Write-Host "" Write-Host "================================================================" Write-Host " Azure RBAC OrgPath Group Provisioning — Complete" Write-Host "================================================================" Write-Host " Groups created : \$(\$results.GroupsCreated)" Write-Host " Groups existing : \$(\$results.GroupsExisting)" Write-Host " RBAC assigned : \$(\$results.RbacAssigned)" Write-Host " Failed : \$(\$results.Failed)" if (\$DryRun) { Write-Host " \*\*\* DRY RUN — no changes were made \*\*\*" } Write-Host "================================================================" Disconnect-MgGraph

### RBAC Scope Strategy

The Scope parameter accepts any valid Azure Resource Manager scope string, and the appropriate scope depends on how Azure resources are organized in the environment. Organizations that have structured their Azure resource hierarchy to mirror OrgTree — creating a dedicated resource group for each organizational unit's resources — should set the scope to the resource group corresponding to each node. At resource-group scope, the RBAC assignment grants the specified role only to resources within that group, which produces a tight, auditable, organizational-boundary-aligned access control boundary. The dynamic group provides the identity filtering; the resource group scope provides the resource filtering; together they form a precise two-dimensional access control specification.

Organizations with a flatter Azure resource organization — where resources from multiple organizational units coexist within the same resource group or subscription — should set the scope at the subscription or management group level and accept that the dynamic group provides the primary access boundary. In this model, all users in the Finance node receive Reader access to all resources in the subscription, not just Finance-owned resources. This is a broader grant than the resource-group-scoped model, but it may be appropriate during the transition period before Azure resources are reorganized to mirror OrgTree. The pipeline is designed to accommodate scope changes without recreating groups: the scope parameter can be updated in the pipeline configuration, and the script will create new role assignments at the new scope without disturbing the dynamic group or its existing membership rule.

The two scope models can coexist within a single deployment. A single invocation of the script with a subscription-level scope provides broad coverage for all nodes. Subsequent invocations with resource-group scopes for specific high-sensitivity nodes layer more restrictive assignments on top of the broad baseline, because a more specific RBAC assignment at resource-group scope does not override or conflict with a broader assignment at subscription scope — RBAC in Azure ARM is additive within a scope hierarchy. The governance team can use this layering to implement a progressively refined access model as resource organization catches up to the OrgTree structure.

<table style="width:1%;">
<colgroup>
<col style="width: 1%" />
</colgroup>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><p><strong>Pipeline Invocation Pattern for Multiple Roles</strong></p>
<p>Run the script once per role definition per scope. A typical initial deployment for a single organizational segment might include three invocations: Reader at the subscription scope, Contributor at the node's dedicated resource group, and Virtual Machine Contributor at the resource group containing Arc-enrolled servers for that segment. The pipeline configuration file should enumerate all role/scope combinations and drive the script invocations in sequence. Adding a new role to the governance model requires adding a new entry to the pipeline configuration file and committing the change — the next pipeline run will create the new groups and assignments for all active nodes automatically.</p></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>

## Chapter 6 — The Closed Loop — What the Full Integration Delivers

### What the Complete Model Enables

When all five parts of this series have been implemented, the organization has something that did not exist before: a unified structural governance model that reaches from the HR system through the identity directory, through privileged access management, through lifecycle automation, through the monitoring plane, and through resource-plane access control — and that does so through a single shared concept. OrgPath is not a tool. It is not a policy. It is not a workflow. It is a piece of metadata that encodes where an identity or resource sits in the organizational hierarchy and makes that position queryable, enforceable, and automatable across every layer of the Microsoft platform stack simultaneously.

The complete model makes possible a class of operational questions that were previously unanswerable from a unified data source. A governance team can now ask: show me every identity, every device, and every server in the Finance division, along with their current PIM eligibilities and their Azure RBAC assignments. That query is answerable from the Sentinel log model, because every asset in the Finance division carries an OrgPath value that places it in the Finance node, and every PIM and RBAC event carries the initiating user's OrgPath or the targeted resource's OrgPath tag. The answer does not require correlating data from four separate administrative consoles — it requires one Sentinel workbook that joins the AuditLogs table to the AzureActivity table on OrgPath values.

An incident response team can now ask: this server was accessed by a user who activated a Tier0 control plane role — does that user's OrgPath entitle them to Tier0 access in this server's organizational segment? That question is answerable by comparing the user's current OrgPath attribute value to the server's OrgPath resource tag and to the PIM eligibility group that corresponds to the intersection of that OrgPath and the Tier0 role. If the user's OrgPath places them in the same organizational branch as the server's OrgPath tag, and they hold eligibility in the corresponding ORG-BRANCH group, the access is structurally consistent. If their OrgPath is from a different segment, the access is structurally anomalous — not necessarily malicious, but requiring explanation and documentation.

A compliance team can now ask: show me every user whose PIM eligibility does not match their current organizational position. That question is answered by Query Three from Chapter Four, running on its daily schedule, with results available in Sentinel as incidents and committed to the governance repository as drift reports. The compliance team does not need to export data from PIM, cross-reference it against a directory dump, and compare it to an organizational chart in a spreadsheet. The answer is a number, updated daily, committed to a version-controlled repository, with a complete audit trail of every previous value and every remediation action taken against each drift event.

The complete integration also enables proactive posture management that was impossible before. Because OrgPath is the common key across all four planes — identity, PIM, monitoring, resource — the governance team can propagate a structural change through all four planes in a single pipeline run. When a new organizational unit is added to OrgTree, the pipeline creates its dynamic group, stamps OrgPath values for all users in that unit, creates its PIM eligibility group and schedule, creates its RBAC dynamic groups and role assignments, and updates the Sentinel workbook's OrgPath filter list — all from the same registry commit, in one automated sequence, without a change ticket for each downstream system.

### What the Model Still Requires

The governance substrate is not maintenance-free, and the engineering team should understand exactly what it still requires before moving into steady-state operations. The OrgTree registry is the foundation of the entire model. If the registry does not reflect the current organizational structure, every downstream system — dynamic groups, PIM eligibilities, RBAC assignments, Sentinel queries — will reflect the outdated structure. The registry must be updated when the organization restructures, and that update must flow through the pipeline before the organizational change takes effect for the people involved. A reorganization that changes OrgPath values without a corresponding registry update creates drift at the source, which propagates outward faster than any pipeline can remediate it. The process discipline for keeping the registry current is as important as the technical pipeline that reads from it.

The pipeline itself must be monitored for failures. Every step in the propagation sequence — OrgPath writes, dynamic group provisioning, PIM eligibility requests, RBAC assignments — can fail independently, and a failure in any step leaves a subset of identities or resources in an inconsistent governance state. The pipeline should write structured output to the governance repository after every run, with a clear status indicator for each node and each operation type. A monitoring alert should fire when any pipeline step produces a non-zero failure count, and the governance team should have a documented remediation procedure for each failure category — what to do when a PIM eligibility request fails because the service principal lacks the required permission, what to do when a group creation fails because the display name conflicts with an existing group, what to do when an RBAC assignment fails because the scope has been deleted.

The Sentinel alert thresholds for the high-risk activation query must be tuned over time. Initial deployment will produce false positives as the team learns what normal PIM activation patterns look like for each organizational segment. A sensitive segment that has routine administrative operations involving Tier0 role activations will generate a high alert volume in the first weeks of monitoring. The governance team should document the expected pattern for each sensitive segment and adjust the query parameters — the lookback window, the justification string validation, the time-of-day filter — to reduce noise without reducing signal. Alert fatigue from untimed thresholds is the most common failure mode of security monitoring programs; it is addressed by tuning, not by silence.

The Lifecycle Workflow mover pattern must be validated against the HR system integration end-to-end before it can be relied upon for compliance-critical access realignment. The validation process involves creating a test identity, recording a position change event in the HR system, tracing the event through the HR provisioning connector to the OrgTree registry update, through the registry update to the OrgPath propagation pipeline, through the OrgPath attribute write to the Lifecycle Workflow trigger, and through the workflow execution to the PIM eligibility change and the access review creation. Every link in that chain must be verified to behave correctly and within the organization's acceptable latency window for access realignment. If any link is unreliable, that link must be hardened before the mover workflow is promoted to production.

### Closing: What Was Built and Why It Matters

Part One of this series described a specific architectural absence in the Microsoft identity stack: a flat directory model that assigns access through explicit group membership and role assignment, with no native concept of organizational structure as a governance primitive. The problem this absence creates is not that access control is impossible — clearly, organizations have been governing access in Entra ID and Active Directory for decades. The problem is that access governance at scale requires organizational structure to be a first-class citizen of the governance model. When organizational position must be expressed through dozens of manually maintained groups, each requiring independent lifecycle management, the governance model becomes an operational liability rather than an operational asset. It cannot keep pace with organizational change. It cannot answer structural questions about who has access to what and why. It produces point-in-time snapshots of access state that are accurate at the moment they are generated and increasingly inaccurate thereafter.

OrgTree provides the shared concept that the Microsoft identity stack was missing. It is an organizational hierarchy, version-controlled, uniquely identified at every node, and designed to be the single source of truth about how the organization is structured. OrgPath provides the expression of that concept on every asset in the environment. It is a single attribute value, uniform in format, readable by any system that can query the directory or read an Azure resource tag. The pipeline propagates both continuously, ensuring that structural reality in the registry produces structural reality in the directory and in the resource plane. The PIM integration makes structural position operationally meaningful for privileged access: where an identity sits determines what it can activate, and that determination updates automatically as position changes. The Lifecycle Workflows make the model resilient across structural change: movers do not accumulate stale privilege because a workflow catches the OrgPath transition and drives remediation without human intervention. Sentinel makes the model visible in the monitoring plane: organizational structure is no longer invisible to the analysts who watch for privilege abuse and anomalous access patterns. Azure RBAC makes the model consistent across the resource plane: the servers and services the organization operates are governed by the same structural logic as the identities that operate them.

What was described in Part One as an architectural absence has been addressed not by replacing the Microsoft platform, but by building the substrate it was missing — and by wiring that substrate into every layer of the security operations model that depends on organizational structure to function at its designed capability. The platform tools are capable. They were waiting for a shared concept of organizational structure to govern against. OrgTree and OrgPath are that concept. The work described across these five documents is the work of giving the platform what it needs to govern at the scale and precision that modern enterprise environments require.

### Appendix: Implementation Artifact Reference

The following table summarizes every implementation artifact introduced in this document, its role in the operational model, and the chapter in which it is defined.

| **Artifact** | **Type** | **Chapter** | **Pipeline Step / Trigger** | **Required Permissions** |
|----|----|----|----|----|
| Set-PIMEligibleGroupsFromOrgPath.ps1 | PowerShell script | 2 | OrgTree commit propagation pipeline, step 4 | Directory.ReadWrite.All, RoleManagement.ReadWrite.Directory |
| Mover Lifecycle Workflow definition | Entra ID Lifecycle Workflow | 3 | OrgPath attribute change event or daily scheduled evaluation | Lifecycle Workflow administrator |
| Mover PIM Realignment Handler (run.ps1) | Azure Function (PowerShell) | 3 | HTTP POST from Lifecycle Workflow custom task extension | Directory.Read.All, RoleManagement.ReadWrite.Directory, AccessReview.ReadWrite.All (Managed Identity) |
| KQL Query 1 — PIM Activations by OrgPath | Sentinel Workbook query | 4 | Workbook tile, refreshed hourly | Log Analytics Reader on connected workspace |
| KQL Query 2 — High-Risk Activations | Sentinel Analytics Rule | 4 | Scheduled rule, 1-hour frequency | Microsoft Sentinel Contributor |
| KQL Query 3 — PIM Eligibility Drift | Sentinel scheduled query / alert | 4 | Daily scheduled query, incident on threshold breach | Microsoft Sentinel Contributor |
| Arc Machine Tag Enforcement Policy | Azure Policy definition | 5 | Deny effect at Arc enrollment API call | Resource Policy Contributor at assignment scope |
| Set-AzRbacOrgPathGroups.ps1 | PowerShell script | 5 | OrgTree commit propagation pipeline, invoked per role/scope | Directory.ReadWrite.All, Group.ReadWrite.All, Owner or User Access Administrator on RBAC scope |

*OrgTree / OrgPath Governance Series — Part Five of Five.*\
Parts One through Four: The Governance Gap in the Microsoft Identity Stack \| The Governance Substrate Requirements \| OrgTree and OrgPath Design \| Implementation Guide for Building the Substrate.\
Document prepared May 8, 2026. For internal engineering use. All environment-specific values (tenant IDs, application IDs, scope paths, role names) must be substituted before executing any script or deploying any policy definition in a production environment.
